Description: A vertical scrolling text screensaver. It provides "digital rain", inspired by the 1999 film "The Matrix". The "droplets" are lines of green characters that fall from the top of the terminal to the bottom, and are occasionally broken up.
Extracts or lists files from a given .a, .ar or .lib archive.
Reports the computer's processor architecture. For example, "i486", "i686", "x86_64", etc.

Name: GNU assembler
Description: An x86 assembler and the default backend of GCC.
Prints a table containing all 128 ASCII characters. For each character, its decimal (Dec) and hexadecimal (Hex) values are displayed with it.
An AWK processor. AWK is a scripting language designed for text pattern scanning and processing and useful for data extraction and reporting.

Extracts the file name and/or the extension from a given path. If just a directory path is given, it will extract both. If a file extension is also given, it will extract the extension in addition to the directory path if it matches. For example, running "basename /etc/resolv.conf" will return "resolv.conf"; running "basename /etc/resolv.conf .conf" will return "resolv".
Used for concatenating (combining) files, providing a standard output for printing or writing to a file. When its output is not directed, it prints the result.

Changes the active virtual terminal. Changing the current virtual terminal is useful for terminal-based multi-tasking, troubleshooting and recovery. It functions similarly to pressing Ctrl+Alt+[F1/F2/F3/etc.]
Clears the terminal so that the cursor is positioned at the top. It is similar to "cls" on MS-DOS, Microsoft Windows, etc.
Compares two files line-by-line and outputs a difference listing between them. The result can be used as a patch file for the patch utility.
Converts a text file from using DOS-style line breaks (carriage return + line feed) to using Unix-style line breaks (line feed).
Identifies and reports the type of data contained in a file.

Reports the amount of total, used and free system physical memory and (if available) swap memory, largely acting as a frontend to /proc/meminfo. It produces a table containing the total (MemTotal/SwapTotal), used (total - available), free (MemFree/SwapFree), shared (Shmem), buffers (Buffers) + cache (Cached/SReclaimable), and available (MemAvailable) memory. All values are in kibibytes (KiB).
An FTP client for remote file transfer.

Name: GCC C++
Description: GCC's C++ compiler.
Licence: GNU GPLv3 + GCC Runtime Library Exception

Name: GCC C
Description: GCC's C compiler (configured to use musl).
Licence: GNU GPLv3 + GCC Runtime Library Exception

Description: Gets an extended attribute from the given filesystem object. Extended attributes are metadata that go beyond the standard attributes provided by the host filesystem, allowing more information to be associated with a given filesystem object.

Name: Gfortran
Description: GCC's Fortran compiler.
Licence: GNU GPLv3 + GCC Runtime Library Exception

Description: A distributed version control software system.

An interactive process viewer designed to be an enhancement over top. Compared to top, htop supports scrolling both horizontally and vertically, a coloured UI and colour-coding, CPU and memory usage bars, searching, filtering, sorting, and killing processes.
A WordStar-style text editor.
Displays the contents of a file in the terminal, allowing it to be scrolled line-by-line. It uses vi-style keybinds.

Creates a hard or symbolic link to a given target file or directory. Hard links are files that point to the same inode as the target file, which must be within the same file system, and hard links to directories are generally not supported. Symbolic links are files that point to the target file or directory, which can be on another file system and even target a non-existent file/directory. Deleting the target of a hard link will not delete the data so long as at least one hard link to it exists; deleting the target of a symbolic link will break the link.
Lists the contents of the current or a target directory.

Lists information about all or the specified block devices. By default, it produces a table containing the device name, major number (driver associated with device), minor number (device number for the given driver), device type, whether the device is read only (RO), and its mountpoint (if mounted).

Lists all PCI buses and connected devices. By default, it produces a table containing the PCI bus number, device number for that bus, function number, PCI class code, vendor ID, and device ID. It can optionally also display the kernel driver utilising a given device.
Lists all USB buses and connected devices. It produces a table containing the USB bus number, device number for that bus, vendor ID, and device ID.
A text-based web browser that allows you to access websites from your terminal. It is the oldest web browser that is still actively maintained.
A text editor with Emacs-style keybinds and buffers.

A Python implementation intended for microcontrollers, embedded systems and other constrained platforms. It implements Python 3.4 syntax with select keywords and features from later versions. It is capable of installing and managing mip modules from micropython-lib.
Creates a directory at the given directory path or in the current directory with a given name without a path.
Creates an ext2 file system on the given device or image file.
Creates a FAT32 file system on the given device or image file. The device or image will be readable with Windows 95 OSR2 or newer.
Used to set up a Linux swap area on a given partition or file. It will destroy any data present on the target partition or file.
A free MP3 player designed to be a drop-in replacement for the non-free mpg123.
Controls a magnetic tape drive. It provides typical drive operations such as rewinding, skipping and ejecting.
A Pico-style text editor.
Displays network statistics such as current network connections, network interfaces and routing tables.

Allows you to run a program that is immune to hang up signals and output routed away or redirected to a file. The program can then run even when the host terminal is closed, the user logs out, or if it is run on a remote system and the connection is closed afterwards. It is often paired with an ampersand (&) to also send the command to the background.
Tells the Linux kernel about the presence and numbering of on-disk partitions.
Applies a patch file to an original file to update it with its contents. A patch file contains a line-by-line difference listing between two files produced by diff, git diff, or similar.

Reports the memory map of a given process. It provides the amount of memory used, proportional set sizes (PSS), dirty pages, permissions on map (mode), and mappings (file, anon/allocated or stack). All memory values are in kilobytes (KB).

Name: Spreadsheet Calculator Improvised
Description: A terminal-based spreadsheet editor based on the original sc (spreadsheet calculator) but adds undo/redo, UTF-8, .xlsx workbook import/export, ODS export, clipboard integration, and Lua-based scripting support. It uses Vim-style keybinds.

Name: Dropbear (SCP)
Description: An SCP client (a part of Dropbear) for remote file transfer.

Sets an extended attribute for the given filesystem object. Extended attributes are metadata that go beyond the standard attributes provided by the host filesystem, allowing more information to be associated with a given filesystem object.
A partitioning tool suited for automated and scripted use.

Name: SHORKDIR
Description: A terminal-based file browser. It allows you to surf the file system with Vim-like controls, WASD or arrow keys, open text-based files with an installed editor of your choice, and inspect a file (if file is installed).

Name: SHORKFETCH
Description: A shark-themed Linux fetch tool designed with speed, consistent output, and vintage hardware support in mind. It displays basic system and environment information in a summarised format. It can report the current username, hostname, operating system/distribution, kernel version, system uptime, number of installed packages, screens, desktop environment, window manager, terminal, console size, shell, CPU, GPUs, system memory, swap memory, root partition size, and local IP address.

Name: SHORKGUI
Description: EXPERIMENTAL. Starts the SHORKGUI graphical environment.

Name: SHORKHELP
Description: Provides help and reference information regarding SHORK and included software and tools. It provides a command database and list, guides, and cheatsheets.

Name: SHORKLOCOMOTIVE
Description: A shark-themed take on the sl command that kindly pokes fun at making typos when trying to type "ls". When ran, an ASCII art shark moves across the terminal from left to right.

Name: SHORKMATRIX
Description: A vertical scrolling text screensaver. It is a minimalist, blue-themed take on CMatrix, a terminal program that provides "digital rain". Inspired by the 1999 film "The Matrix", the "droplets" are lines of blue characters that fall from the top of the terminal to the bottom, and are occasionally broken up.

Name: SHORKOFF
Description: A shutdown helper that safely brings the system to a controlled halt before a manual power off. It is intended as an explicit utility that the user can call to sync write cache to help prevent data corruption or loss on especially older hardware when faced with a manual power off, then halts the system so the user can switch the power off without interrupting any active operations.

Name: SHORKSAY
Description: A shark-themed take on the cowsay command that outputs an ASCII art shark and a speech bubble containing a message of your choice. The message can be manually supplied or can be provided from another program's standard output (ie, using a pipe).

Name: SHORKSET
Description: A settings program for changing SHORK's display resolution, keyboard layout (keymap), terminal PSF font, and terminal font colour.

Name: Dropbear (SSH)
Description: An SSH client (a part of Dropbear) for remote login and command-line execution.
Used for initialising SCSI magnetic tape drive modes at system startup, when a tape driver module is loaded, or when a new tape driver is initialised using terminal commands.

Executes a given command to capture and trace its system calls and signals. It is a useful diagnosis and debugging tool one can run if a program fails unexpectedly, and/or without meaningful error messages or logs.
Used to stop memory paging/swapping on one or all swap-capable devices. If used and no swap devices are left online, the computer will be entirely reliant on system memory (RAM) for program memory.

Used to start memory paging/swapping on one or all swap-capable devices. Swapping allows fixed storage (hard disk drrive, solid-state drive, etc.) or removable storage (USB stick, etc.) to be used for program memory in addition to system memory (RAM). It is useful as a buffer for when free RAM runs low, although using swap memory will be much slower than RAM.

Commits any filesystem write operations that are currently cached. It is useful as an immediate and explicit way to ensure data is written to any disks/drives before powering off the computer, removing a drive, etc.
A lightweight C compiler (configured to use musl).

A terminal multiplexer that allows for multi-tasking and multi-viewing with just a single terminal. It allows you to create multiple "windows" to switch between without switching virtual terminals or opening a new window/tab in your terminal emulator, split said "windows" into multiple panes that can present terminal programs and output side-by-side, and quickly switch between them. Each tmux session can also be detached and reattached without losing what is open inside.

A process viewer that provides a real-time view of running processes and their CPU and memory usage. Besides comma-separated memory and CPU statistics and averages, it produces a table containing the process ID (PID), parent process ID (PPID), user, process state (STAT), virtual memory size in kilobytes (VSZ), VSZ as a percentage of total RAM (%VSZ), the last CPU core used, percentage of CPU time used (%CPU) and the executing command for each process that can fit into a single non-scrolling terminal.

Typically used to update the access and modification dates/times of one or more given files. It can also be used to create one or many empty files, as seen in some Linux books and tutorials, although this is often redundant.
Converts a text file from using Unix-style line breaks (line feed) to using DOS-style line breaks (carriage return + line feed).
Generates and prints a universally unique identifier (UUID), a 128-bit value for identification that is practically guaranteed to be unique.

Reports system virtual memory, processes, CPU and I/O statistics. It displays columns for a runnable process count (r), I/O-bound blocked process count (b), swap memory used (swpd), idle memory (free), memory as buffers (buff), memory as cache (cache), memory swapped in from disk (si), memory swapped to disk (so), KiB received from a block device (bi), KiB sent to a block device (bo), interrupts per second (in), context switches per second (cs), user CPU time (us), system CPU time (sy), CPU idle time (id), time waiting for IO (wa), time stolen from a virtual machine (st), and time running KVM guest code (gu).
A non-interactive internet file downloader. It is often used to simply download a file with a given URL, although it can also be used as a web spider.
Locates the binary, source and manual page files for a given command.
